content.content:
1	// Marble base, mostly copied from hw_test.v
2	// Instantiates rtefi_blob and support code
3	// Needs to be kept 100% portable/synthesizable
4	
5	module marble_base (
6		// GMII Tx port
7		input vgmii_tx_clk,
8		output [7:0] vgmii_txd,
9		output vgmii_tx_en,
10		output vgmii_tx_er,
11	
12		// GMII Rx port
13		input vgmii_rx_clk,
14		input [7:0] vgmii_rxd,
15		input vgmii_rx_er,
16		input vgmii_rx_dv,
17	
18		// Auxiliary I/O and status
19		input aux_clk,
20		input clk62,
21		input cfg_clk,
22		output phy_rstn,
23		input clk_locked,
24		input si570,
25	
26		// SPI pins to on-board microcontroller; can give access to configuration
27		input SCLK,
28		input CSB,
29		input MOSI,
30		output MISO,
31		output mmc_int,
32	
33		// SPI boot flash programming port
34		output boot_clk,
35		output boot_cs,
36		output boot_mosi,
37		input boot_miso,
38		output cfg_d02,
39	
40		// One I2C bus; everything gatewayed through a TCA9548A
41		inout  [3:0] twi_scl,
42		inout  [3:0] twi_sda,
43		inout  TWI_RST,
44		input  TWI_INT,
45	
46		// White Rabbit compatible DAC subsystem controlling VCXOs
47		output WR_DAC_SCLK,
48		output WR_DAC_DIN,
49		output WR_DAC1_SYNC,
50		output WR_DAC2_SYNC,
51	
52		// UART to USB
53		// The RxD and TxD directions are with respect
54		// to the USB/UART chip, not the FPGA!
55		// Note that the freq_demo feature doesn't actually use FPGA_TxD.
56		// If you don't connect anything to FPGA_RxD, the synthesizer
57		// will drop the whole freq_demo feature.
58		output FPGA_RxD,
59		input FPGA_TxD,
60	
61		// Digilent GPS
62		input [3:0] GPS,
63	
64		// Placeholder for configuring external devices
65		output [3:0] ext_config,
66	
67		// Simulation-only, please ignore in synthesis
68		output in_use,
69	
70		// Local bus for an external application
71		// Define clock domain
72		output lb_clk,
73		output [23:0] lb_addr,
74		output lb_strobe,
75		output lb_rd,
76		output lb_write,
77		output lb_rd_valid,
78		// output [read_pipe_len:0] control_pipe_rd,
79		output [31:0] lb_data_out,
80		input [31:0] lb_data_in,
81	
82		// Something physical
83		inout [191:0] fmc_test,
84		output ZEST_PWR_EN,
85		output [2:0] ps_sync,
86		output [7:0] LED
87	);
88	
89	parameter USE_I2CBRIDGE = 1;
90	parameter MMC_CTRACE = 1;
91	parameter GPS_CTRACE = 0;
92	parameter use_ddr_pps = 0;
93	parameter misc_config_default = 0;
94	parameter default_enable_rx = 1;
95	
96	`ifdef VERILATOR
97	parameter [31:0] ip = {8'd192, 8'd168, 8'd7, 8'd4};  // 192.168.7.4
98	parameter [47:0] mac = 48'h12555500012d;
99	`else
100	`ifdef YOSYS
101	parameter [31:0] ip = {8'd192, 8'd168, 8'd19, 8'd9};  // 192.168.19.9
102	parameter [47:0] mac = 48'h12555500022d;
103	`else
104	parameter [31:0] ip = {8'd192, 8'd168, 8'd19, 8'd10};  // 192.168.19.10
105	parameter [47:0] mac = 48'h12555500032d;
106	`endif
107	`endif
108	
109	// Basic clock setup
110	wire tx_clk = vgmii_tx_clk;
111	wire rx_clk = vgmii_rx_clk;
112	
113	// Local bus
114	wire lb_control_strobe, lb_control_rd, lb_control_rd_valid;  // outputs from rtefi_blob
115	assign lb_clk = tx_clk;
116	assign lb_strobe = lb_control_strobe;
117	wire config_clk = tx_clk;
118	assign lb_write = lb_control_strobe & ~lb_control_rd;
119	assign lb_rd_valid = lb_control_rd_valid;
120	assign lb_rd = lb_control_rd;
121	
122	// Signals provided by mmc_mailbox
123	wire [3:0] spi_pins_debug;
124	wire enable_rx;
125	wire [7:0] mbox_out2;
126	wire config_s, config_p;
127	wire [7:0] config_a, config_d;
128	
129	// Actual mmc_mailbox instance
130	mmc_mailbox #(
131	  .DEFAULT_ENABLE_RX(default_enable_rx)
132	  ) mailbox_i (
133	  .clk(config_clk), // input
134	  // localbus
135	  .lb_addr(lb_addr[10:0]), // input [10:0]
136	  .lb_din(lb_data_out[7:0]), // input [7:0]
137	  .lb_dout(mbox_out2), // output [7:0]
138	  .lb_write(lb_write), // input
139	  .lb_control_strobe(lb_control_strobe), // input
140	  // SPI PHY
141	  .sck(SCLK), // input
142	  .ncs(CSB), // input
143	  .pico(MOSI), // input
144	  .poci(MISO), // output
145	  // Config pins for badger (rtefi) interface
146	  .config_s(config_s), // output
147	  .config_p(config_p), // output
148	  .config_a(config_a), // output [7:0]
149	  .config_d(config_d), // output [7:0]
150	  // Special pins
151	  .enable_rx(enable_rx), // output
152	  .spi_pins_debug(spi_pins_debug) // {MISO, din, sclk_d1, csb_d1};
153	);
154	
155	// Debugging hooks
156	wire ibadge_stb, obadge_stb;
157	wire [7:0] ibadge_data, obadge_data;
158	wire xdomain_fault;
159	wire tx_mac_done;
160	wire [15:0] rx_mac_data;
161	wire rx_mac_hbank;
162	wire [1:0] rx_mac_buf_status;
163	wire allow_mmc_eth_config;
164	wire [31:0] lb_slave_data_read;
165	
166	// for looking at start-up frequency of SI570
167	wire [27:0] frequency_si570;
168	freq_count freq_cnt_si570(.f_in(si570), .sysclk(lb_clk), .frequency(frequency_si570));
169	
170	// Signals provided to lb_marble_slave
171	wire [3:0] rx_category_rx, rx_category;
172	wire rx_category_s_rx, rx_category_s;
173	
174	// Signals provided by lb_marble_slave
175	wire [1:0] led_user_mode;
176	wire l1, l2;
177	wire [4:0] ps_sync_config;
178	
179	// Actual lb_marble_slave instance
180	lb_marble_slave #(
181		.USE_I2CBRIDGE(USE_I2CBRIDGE),
182		.MMC_CTRACE(MMC_CTRACE),
183		.GPS_CTRACE(GPS_CTRACE),
184		.use_ddr_pps(use_ddr_pps),
185		.misc_config_default(misc_config_default)
186	) slave(
187		.clk(lb_clk), .addr(lb_addr),
188		.control_strobe(lb_control_strobe), .control_rd(lb_control_rd),
189		.data_out(lb_data_out), .data_in(lb_slave_data_read),
190		.aux_clk(aux_clk), .clk62(clk62),
191		.ibadge_clk(rx_clk),
192		.ibadge_stb(ibadge_stb), .ibadge_data(ibadge_data),
193		.obadge_stb(obadge_stb), .obadge_data(obadge_data),
194		.xdomain_fault(xdomain_fault),
195		.mmc_pins(spi_pins_debug),
196		.rx_category_s(rx_category_s), .rx_category(rx_category),
197		.tx_mac_done(tx_mac_done), .rx_mac_data(rx_mac_data),
198		.rx_mac_buf_status(rx_mac_buf_status), .rx_mac_hbank(rx_mac_hbank),
199		.twi_scl(twi_scl), .twi_sda(twi_sda),
200		.twi_int(TWI_INT), .twi_rst(TWI_RST),
201		.wr_dac_sclk(WR_DAC_SCLK), .wr_dac_sdo(WR_DAC_DIN),
202		.wr_dac_sync({WR_DAC2_SYNC, WR_DAC1_SYNC}),
203		.cfg_d02(cfg_d02),
204		.mmc_int(mmc_int),
205		.zest_pwr_en(ZEST_PWR_EN),
206		.allow_mmc_eth_config(allow_mmc_eth_config),
207		.fmc_test(fmc_test),
208		.gps(GPS), .ext_config(ext_config), .frequency_si570(frequency_si570),
209		.ps_sync_config(ps_sync_config),
210		.led_user_mode(led_user_mode), .led1(l1), .led2(l2)
211	);
212	
213	// Delegate part of the address space to application code outside this module
214	reg [23:0] p3_lb_addr_d;
215	reg p3_use_app_rd=0, p3_use_mbox_rd=0;
216	always @(posedge lb_clk) begin
217		p3_lb_addr_d <= lb_addr;
218		p3_use_app_rd <= p3_lb_addr_d[23:20] == 1;
219		p3_use_mbox_rd <= p3_lb_addr_d[23:20] == 2;
220	end
221	wire [31:0] p3_lb_data_in = p3_use_mbox_rd ? mbox_out2 : p3_use_app_rd ? lb_data_in : lb_slave_data_read;
222	
223	// MAC master
224	// Clearly not useful in the long run to drive this only from
225	// the localbus, but doing so makes for a much lighter-weight test
226	// than installing a real soft-core CPU.
227	parameter tx_mac_aw=10;
228	wire host_clk = lb_clk;
229	wire host_write = lb_control_strobe & ~lb_control_rd & (lb_addr[23:20]==1);
230	wire [tx_mac_aw:0] host_waddr = lb_addr[tx_mac_aw:0];
231	wire [15:0] host_wdata = lb_data_out[15:0];
232	wire [10:0] host_raddr = lb_addr[10:0];  // for Rx MAC
233	
234	// Rx MAC with DPRAM
235	wire [7:0] rx_mac_d;
236	wire [11:0] rx_mac_a;
237	wire rx_mac_wen;
238	wire rx_mac_accept, rx_mac_status_s;
239	wire [7:0] rx_mac_status_d;
240	base_rx_mac rx_mac(
241		// host access
242		.host_clk(host_clk), .host_raddr(host_raddr),
243		.host_rdata(rx_mac_data),
244		// connection to Rx MAC port
245		.rx_clk(vgmii_rx_clk),
246		.rx_mac_d(rx_mac_d), .rx_mac_a(rx_mac_a), .rx_mac_wen(rx_mac_wen),
247		.rx_mac_accept(rx_mac_accept),
248		.rx_mac_status_d(rx_mac_status_d), .rx_mac_status_s(rx_mac_status_s)
249	);
250	
251	// memory and control signals are handled external to mac_subset.v
252	// in this branch, which the testbenches were not designed for ...
253	// the next few lines are patching that up
254	wire [tx_mac_aw-1:0] host_tx_raddr;
255	wire [15:0] host_tx_rdata;
256	wire [tx_mac_aw-1:0] buf_start_addr;
257	wire tx_mac_start;
258	mac_compat_dpram #(
259		.mac_aw(tx_mac_aw)
260	) mac_compat_dpram_inst (
261		.host_clk(host_clk),
262		.host_waddr(host_waddr),
263		.host_write(host_write),
264		.host_wdata(host_wdata),
265	// ----------------------------
266		.tx_clk(tx_clk),
267		.host_raddr(host_tx_raddr),
268		.host_rdata(host_tx_rdata),
269		.buf_start_addr(buf_start_addr),
270		.tx_mac_start(tx_mac_start)
271	);
272	
273	// Be careful with clock domains:
274	// rtefi_blob uses this in rx_clk, so send it a registered value.
275	reg enable_rx_r=0;  always @(posedge tx_clk) enable_rx_r <= enable_rx | ~allow_mmc_eth_config;
276	
277	// Instantiate the Real Work
278	parameter enable_bursts=1;
279	
280	wire [3:0] scanner_debug;
281	wire rx_mon, tx_mon;
282	wire boot_busy, blob_in_use;
283	rtefi_blob #(.ip(ip), .mac(mac), .mac_aw(tx_mac_aw), .p3_enable_bursts(enable_bursts)) rtefi(
284		.rx_clk(vgmii_rx_clk), .rxd(vgmii_rxd),
285		.rx_dv(vgmii_rx_dv), .rx_er(vgmii_rx_er),
286		.tx_clk(tx_clk) , .txd(vgmii_txd),
287		.tx_en(vgmii_tx_en),  // no vgmii_tx_er
288	
289	// Note special-case enabling of config controls from MMC!
290	// Expect allow_mmc_eth_config to be 1 in the long run,
291	// but the 0 case is very interesting for debugging, and in fact
292	// is the power-on default for now.
293	// Simple combinational logic is OK, since all signals are in tx_clk domain
294	// (config_clk == tx_clk == lb_clk).
295	
296		.enable_rx(enable_rx_r),
297		.config_clk(config_clk),
298		.config_s(config_s & allow_mmc_eth_config),
299		.config_p(config_p & allow_mmc_eth_config),
300		.config_a(config_a[3:0]), .config_d(config_d),
301	
302		// .host_clk(host_clk), .host_write(host_write),
303		// .host_waddr(host_waddr), .host_wdata(host_wdata),
304		.host_raddr(host_tx_raddr),
305		.host_rdata(host_tx_rdata),
306		.buf_start_addr(buf_start_addr),
307		.tx_mac_start(tx_mac_start),
308	
309		.tx_mac_done(tx_mac_done),
310		.rx_mac_d(rx_mac_d), .rx_mac_a(rx_mac_a), .rx_mac_wen(rx_mac_wen),
311		.rx_mac_hbank(rx_mac_hbank), .rx_mac_buf_status(rx_mac_buf_status),
312		.rx_mac_accept(rx_mac_accept),
313		.rx_mac_status_d(rx_mac_status_d), .rx_mac_status_s(rx_mac_status_s),
314		.scanner_debug(scanner_debug),
315		.ibadge_stb(ibadge_stb), .ibadge_data(ibadge_data),
316		.obadge_stb(obadge_stb), .obadge_data(obadge_data),
317		.xdomain_fault(xdomain_fault),
318		.p2_nomangle(1'b0),
319		.p3_addr(lb_addr), .p3_control_strobe(lb_control_strobe),
320		.p3_control_rd(lb_control_rd), .p3_control_rd_valid(lb_control_rd_valid),
321		.p3_data_out(lb_data_out), .p3_data_in(p3_lb_data_in),
322		.p4_spi_clk(boot_clk), .p4_spi_cs(boot_cs),
323		.p4_spi_mosi(boot_mosi), .p4_spi_miso(boot_miso),
324		.p4_busy(boot_busy),
325		.rx_mon(rx_mon), .tx_mon(tx_mon), .in_use(blob_in_use)
326	);
327	assign vgmii_tx_er=1'b0;
328	assign in_use = blob_in_use | boot_busy;
329	
330	// Frequency counter demo to UART
331	wire [3:0] unk_clk = {cfg_clk, si570, aux_clk, rx_clk};
332	freq_demo freq_demo(
333		.refclk(tx_clk), .unk_clk(unk_clk),
334		.uart_tx(FPGA_RxD), .uart_rx(FPGA_TxD)
335	);
336	
337	// For statistics-gathering purposes
338	packet_categorize i_categorize(.clk(vgmii_rx_clk),
339		.strobe(rx_mac_status_s), .status(rx_mac_status_d),
340		.strobe_o(rx_category_s_rx), .category(rx_category_rx)
341	);
342	data_xdomain #(.size(4)) x_category(
343		.clk_in(vgmii_rx_clk), .gate_in(rx_category_s_rx), .data_in(rx_category_rx),
344		.clk_out(lb_clk), .gate_out(rx_category_s), .data_out(rx_category)
345	);
346	
347	// Heartbeats and other LED
348	reg [26:0] rx_heartbeat=0, tx_heartbeat=0;
349	always @(posedge rx_clk) rx_heartbeat <= rx_heartbeat+1;
350	always @(posedge tx_clk) tx_heartbeat <= tx_heartbeat+1;
351	wire led0 = led_user_mode ? l1 : rx_heartbeat[26];
352	wire led1 = led_user_mode ? l2 : tx_heartbeat[26];
353	wire rx_led, tx_led;
354	activity rx_act(.clk(rx_clk), .trigger(rx_mon), .led(rx_led));
355	activity tx_act(.clk(tx_clk), .trigger(tx_mon), .led(tx_led));
356	wire rx_h = rx_heartbeat[26];
357	wire tx_h = tx_heartbeat[26];
358	reg mod=0, reset=0;
359	reg [31:0] cnt=0;
360	always @(posedge rx_clk) begin
361	    cnt <= reset ? 32'h0 : cnt + 1'b1;
362	    mod <= cnt[0];
363	end
364	assign LED = (led_user_mode==2) ? cnt[30:23] & {8{mod}} : {~tx_h, tx_h, ~rx_h, rx_h, tx_led, rx_led, led1, led0};
365	
366	// Keep the PHY's reset pin low for the first 33 ms
367	reg phy_rb=0;
368	always @(posedge tx_clk) begin
369		if (tx_heartbeat[21]) phy_rb <= 1;
370		if (~clk_locked) phy_rb <= 0;
371	end
372	assign phy_rstn = phy_rb;
373	
374	ltm_sync ltm_sync_i(.clk(tx_clk),
375		.ps_config(ps_sync_config), .ps_sync(ps_sync));
376	
377	// One weird hack, even works in Verilator!
378	`ifndef YOSYS
379	always @(posedge tx_clk) begin
380		if (slave.stop_sim & ~in_use) begin
381			$display("marble_base:  stopping based on localbus request");
382			$finish(0);
383		end
384	end
385	`endif
386	
387	endmodule
388

1	## Context
2	Marble-Mini is an Open Hardware FMC carrier board based on Xilinx Artix:
3	  see [github](https://github.com/BerkeleyLab/Marble-Mini)
4	
5	Zest is an Open Hardware FMC mezzanine board with eight 125 MS/s ADC channels and two 250 MS/s DAC channels:
6	  see [github](https://github.com/BerkeleyLab/Zest)
7	
8	## Firmware related info
9	prc_common.xdc
10	  comes from gitlab.lbl.gov:llrf-projects/lcls2_llrf
11	  firmware/prc/prc_common.xdc
12	
13	digitizer_digital_pin.txt  comes from gitlab.lbl.gov/hardware-designs/llrf5_board_design
14	  board/digitizer/digitizer_digital_pin.txt
15	  (Zest)
16	  this is source; digitizer_02_fmc.sch was machine-generated from it
17	
18	fmc-hpc.lst
19	fmc-lpc.lst
20	  come from gitlab.lbl.gov:hdl-libraries/board-support
21	  bmb7_kintex/fmc-hpc.lst
22	  bmb7_kintex/fmc-lpc.lst
23	
24	BMB-7 naming convention:
25	  fmc-lpc is FMC1 is P1
26	  fmc-hpc is FMC2 is P2
27	
28	Due to different P2 bank voltages for BMB-7 and Marble,
29	  need to map LVCMOS18 to LVCMOS25
30	  and LVDS to LVDS_25
31	(the P1 bank voltage is 2.5V in both cases)
32	
33	example column output:
34	  prc_name              bmb7_iostandard  bmb7_pin  fmc_name     zest_pin
35	  bus_digitizer_U3[16]  LVDS_25          H23       FMC1_LA22_P  ADC_D0A_N_1
36	
37	
38	## Build FW and program Marble
39	```
40	cd $BEDROCK/projects/oscope/marble_family
41	make clean && make oscope_top.bit
42	openocd -f ../../../board_support/marblemini/marble.cfg -c "transport select jtag; init; xc7_program xc7.tap; pld load 0 oscope_top.bit; exit"
43	```
44	Check if it's up
45	```
46	ping 192.168.19.8
47	```
48	
49	## Zest related initialization
50	### Clock frequency
51	Currently OSCOPE lb/system clock works in 125MHz domain.
52	
53	With below ADC clock domain can be set to 100MHz
54	
55	LMK dividers in zest_setup.py for zest are set to divide the below 1400MHz down to 100MHz [This can be changed Inside zest_setup.py .. good luck]
56	For waveforms, using Si***_loader from:
57	https://github.com/michael-betz/Si5xx-5x7-EVV_autoloader
58	```
59	python setFreq.py -p /dev/ttyUSB2 156.25e6 1400e6
60	```
61	
62	### PYTHONPATH setup
63	
64	Setup the right python path for software dependencies sprayed around bedrock. And bmb7 programming tools are best to get from lcls2 as they keep them up-to-date, since they have no choice.
65	
66	```
67	cd $BEDROCK/projects/oscope/software
68	export PYTHONPATH=../../../dsp/:../../common:../../../board_support/zest:/home/w/work/lbl/lcls2_llrf/software/bmb7
69	```
70	
71	### Configure zest and run oscope
72	Setup idelays etc on zest with zest_setup.py
73	```
74	python ../../../board_support/zest/zest_setup.py -a 192.168.19.8:803 -r -f 125
75	python ../software/main.py -a 192.168.19.8:803
76	```
77

file.content:
## Context
Marble-Mini is an Open Hardware FMC carrier board based on Xilinx Artix:
  see [github](https://github.com/BerkeleyLab/Marble-Mini)

Zest is an Open Hardware FMC mezzanine board with eight 125 MS/s ADC channels and two 250 MS/s DAC channels:
  see [github](https://github.com/BerkeleyLab/Zest)

## Firmware related info
prc_common.xdc
  comes from gitlab.lbl.gov:llrf-projects/lcls2_llrf
  firmware/prc/prc_common.xdc

digitizer_digital_pin.txt  comes from gitlab.lbl.gov/hardware-designs/llrf5_board_design
  board/digitizer/digitizer_digital_pin.txt
  (Zest)
  this is source; digitizer_02_fmc.sch was machine-generated from it

fmc-hpc.lst
fmc-lpc.lst
  come from gitlab.lbl.gov:hdl-libraries/board-support
  bmb7_kintex/fmc-hpc.lst
  bmb7_kintex/fmc-lpc.lst

BMB-7 naming convention:
  fmc-lpc is FMC1 is P1
  fmc-hpc is FMC2 is P2

Due to different P2 bank voltages for BMB-7 and Marble,
  need to map LVCMOS18 to LVCMOS25
  and LVDS to LVDS_25
(the P1 bank voltage is 2.5V in both cases)

example column output:
  prc_name              bmb7_iostandard  bmb7_pin  fmc_name     zest_pin
  bus_digitizer_U3[16]  LVDS_25          H23       FMC1_LA22_P  ADC_D0A_N_1


## Build FW and program Marble
```
cd $BEDROCK/projects/oscope/marble_family
make clean && make oscope_top.bit
openocd -f ../../../board_support/marblemini/marble.cfg -c "transport select jtag; init; xc7_program xc7.tap; pld load 0 oscope_top.bit; exit"
```
Check if it's up
```
ping 192.168.19.8
```

## Zest related initialization
### Clock frequency
Currently OSCOPE lb/system clock works in 125MHz domain.

With below ADC clock domain can be set to 100MHz

LMK dividers in zest_setup.py for zest are set to divide the below 1400MHz down to 100MHz [This can be changed Inside zest_setup.py .. good luck]
For waveforms, using Si***_loader from:
https://github.com/michael-betz/Si5xx-5x7-EVV_autoloader
```
python setFreq.py -p /dev/ttyUSB2 156.25e6 1400e6
```

### PYTHONPATH setup

Setup the right python path for software dependencies sprayed around bedrock. And bmb7 programming tools are best to get from lcls2 as they keep them up-to-date, since they have no choice.

```
cd $BEDROCK/projects/oscope/software
export PYTHONPATH=../../../dsp/:../../common:../../../board_support/zest:/home/w/work/lbl/lcls2_llrf/software/bmb7
```

### Configure zest and run oscope
Setup idelays etc on zest with zest_setup.py
```
python ../../../board_support/zest/zest_setup.py -a 192.168.19.8:803 -r -f 125
python ../software/main.py -a 192.168.19.8:803
```

aiTitle: Bedrock 工程集成实战讲义生成

content.thinking:
Now I have a very rich picture of `marble_base`. This is the core integration. Key insights:

1. `marble_top.v` → thin FPGA pin wrapper, includes `marble_mid.vh`
2. `marble_mid.vh` → clock generation + `gmii_to_rgmii` + instantiates `marble_base`
3. `marble_base_shell.v` → CDC boundary shell (registers signals, marks `magic_cdc`)
4. `marble_base.v` (in board_support/marblemini) → the REAL integration:
   - `mmc_mailbox` - SPI mailbox to microcontroller (provides config_s/config_p/config_a/config_d for badger)
   - `lb_marble_slave` - the localbus slave that handles I2C, GPS, FMC test, etc.
   - `base_rx_mac` / `mac_compat_dpram` - RX/TX MAC with DPRAM
   - `rtefi_blob` - "The Real Work" - the Packet Badger (rtefi = the badger). It has `.p3_addr`, `.p3_control_strobe` etc. — p3 is the localbus passthrough port! This is the key: the UDP packet → badger → p3 → localbus.
   - `freq_demo` - frequency counter demo to UART
   - `data_xdomain` for packet categorization
   - The address space partitioning: `lb_addr[23:20]==1` → app, `==2` → mailbox, else slave

The data path from UDP to localbus register:
- Ethernet RGMII → `gmii_to_rgmii` (in marble_mid) → GMII
- GMII → `rtefi_blob` (badger) `.rxd/.rx_dv` on rx_clk
- rtefi_blob p3 port → drives `lb_control_strobe/lb_control_rd/lb_addr/lb_data_out` on `lb_clk` (=tx_clk)
- The localbus signals go to `lb_marble_slave` (and app code via `lb_data_in`)
- `lb_marble_slave` decodes the address and provides `lb_slave_data_read`

So the key architectural insight: `rtefi_blob` (Packet Badger) is the localbus MASTER. It sources `lb_addr/lb_strobe/lb_data_out`, and the p3 port is the localbus passthrough. The `marble_base` instantiates the master, and `lb_marble_slave` is the application slave that responds.

Now let me look at the wctrace.v (ctrace module) and the oscope_top.sv, plus the test_marble Makefile and trigger_capture. Let me also check the badger `rtefi_blob` to understand p3 port and the `comms_top` to round out the integration examples.

Actually I have enough on the Marble side. Let me focus on:
- wctrace.v (the actual ctrace for the ctrace module minimal module)
- projects/oscope/marble_family/oscope_top.sv (another integration example)
- trigger_capture (it's Python/migen based — litex). Let me check the trigger_capture marble.py to understand it
- test_marble Makefile (build organization)

Let me read these. Also I should understand the `rtefi_blob` to confirm p3 is the localbus port. Let me grep for it.

content.thinking:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding. The oscope_top shows another pattern: same `marble_base` reuse, but with an `application_top` as the application slave (replacing the test `lb_din=32'hfaceface`). This is a key integration insight: `marble_base` exposes a localbus `lb_*` port, and each project attaches its own `application_top` as the slave.

Now let me confirm the `rtefi_blob` p3 port (the localbus passthrough from badger), and look at the trigger_capture (which is a different style — migen/Litex based). Let me also check the test_marble Makefile to understand build targets. Let me grep for rtefi_blob and p3.

Actually, I have strong understanding from marble_base.v already:
- `rtefi_blob` has `.p3_addr(lb_addr), .p3_control_strobe(lb_control_strobe), .p3_control_rd(lb_control_rd), .p3_control_rd_valid(lb_control_rd_valid), .p3_data_out(lb_data_out), .p3_data_in(p3_lb_data_in)` — so p3 is the localbus master port coming OUT of the badger
- The badger drives `lb_addr/lb_strobe/lb_data_out`, and the application provides `lb_data_in`

So the full UDP→localbus path:
1. Ethernet PHY (RGMII pins) 
2. → `gmii_to_rgmii` → GMII (vgmii_*)
3. → `rtefi_blob` (= Packet Badger) `.rxd/.rx_dv` on `rx_clk`
4. → badger parses ARP/ICMP/UDP, for a UDP packet destined to a registered client (like the localbus gateway, port = "client"), it issues a localbus transaction on the p3 port
5. → p3 port drives `lb_addr/lb_control_strobe/lb_control_rd/lb_data_out` (on tx_clk = lb_clk)
6. → `lb_marble_slave` (in test build) or `application_top` (in oscope) decodes address, returns read data via `lb_data_in`/`p3_lb_data_in`

This is the complete read/write data path. 

Let me also confirm: where does `p3` come from in the badger? Let me grep rtefi_blob to confirm it's the badger and p3 is the localbus port. Let me also look at trigger_capture briefly to mention it's a different (migen/LiteX) style, and the test_marble Makefile. Let me also verify the comms_top structure quickly.

file.content:
include ../../dir_list.mk
I2CBRIDGE_DIR = $(PERIPH_DRIVERS_DIR)/i2cbridge
include $(I2CBRIDGE_DIR)/i2cbridge_rules.mk
# Unisims not provided
UNISIMS_DIR = broken

APP_NAME = marble
GTX_TCL :=
# Select between marblemini, marble
# XXX needs support for marble_fiber
CONFIG ?= marblemini
ifeq ($(CONFIG),marblemini)
  BOARD_DIR     := marblemini
  BOARD_TOP_CSV := marble_mini_top.csv
  IP_ADDR       := 192.168.19.31
else ifneq (,$(filter marble,$(CONFIG)))
  BOARD_DIR     := marble
  BOARD_TOP_CSV := marble_top.csv
  SERIAL        ?= ""
  IP_ADDR       := 192.168.19.$(SERIAL)
  ZEST_SN       ?= "LBNL DIGITIZER V1.0 SN  024"
else
  $(error Unknown CONFIG '$(CONFIG)'. Please choose 'marblemini', or 'marble')
endif

# It seems like the variable VERILOG_TB from $(BUILD_DIR)/top_rules.mk is intended to be
# used for this exact recipe.  But other local additions to variables like "VERILOG" cause
# its expansion to now include multiple copies of the same flag/arg so it looks a little
# funny at the command line (doesn't seem to cause trouble).  This should be cleaned up.
# For now, redundantly inserting another "-DSIMULATE" here.
#$(VERILOG_TB)
%_tb: %_tb.v
	$(VERILOG) $(VFLAGS_$@) -DSIMULATE -o $@ $(filter %.v, $^)
%_check: %_tb
	$(VVP) $< $(VVP_FLAGS)

# =====
# Possibly common setup / configuration
include $(BUILD_DIR)/top_rules.mk

# Local overrides of default variables
VERILOG += -Wno-timescale
# VERILOG += -g2005-sv
# Configuration not covered (yet?) by Bedrock's top_rules.mk
VCD_ARGS = $(VCD_ARGS_$@)
VLATOR_LINT_IGNORE += -Wno-UNUSED -Wno-DECLFILENAME

# XXX consider converting this to something more compatible with Bedrock's VIVADO_SYNTH.
VIVADOEXEC = vivado
SYNTH_VIVADO = $(VIVADOEXEC) -mode batch -source

# =====
# Attention!!!!
vpath %.v $(BADGER_DIR)/tests $(HOMELESS_DIR) $(DSP_DIR) $(SERIAL_IO_DIR) $(I2CBRIDGE_DIR) $(PERIPH_DRIVERS_DIR) \
  $(BOARD_SUPPORT_DIR)/marblemini $(HOMELESS_DIR)/freq_demo ./pps_lock $(FPGA_FAMILY) $(FPGA_FAMILY_DIR)/xilinx
vpath %.c $(BADGER_DIR)/tests $(BUILD_DIR)

# =====
# gets us $(FREQ_DEMO_V)
include $(HOMELESS_DIR)/freq_demo/freq_demo_rules.mk

# This must go in front of include $(BADGER_DIR)/rules.mk
all: lb_marble_slave_tb marble_base_tb no_multiple_drivers_check marble_base_lint gps_test_tb ltm_sync_tb Vmarble_base dna_check mmc_mailbox_check

# =====
# gps_test, too simple to need testing ... right
gps_test_tb: gps_test.v simpleuart.v dpram.v

ltm_sync_tb: ltm_sync.v

# Packet Badger synthesizable code
RTEFI_CLIENT_LIST = hello.v speed_test.v mem_gateway.v spi_flash.v
RTEFI_EXTRA_V = spi_flash_engine.v
include $(BADGER_DIR)/rules.mk

# =====
# auto-generated files
scalar_marble_regmap.json: lb_marble_slave.v
	$(PYTHON) $(BUILD_DIR)/reverse_json.py $< > $@
marble_regmap.json: static_regmap.json scalar_marble_regmap.json
	$(PYTHON) $(BUILD_DIR)/merge_json.py -o $@ -i $(filter %.json, $^)
config_romx.v: marble_regmap.json $(filter-out config_romx.v, $(MARBLE_SYNTH_SOURCE))
	$(PYTHON) -m leep.build_rom --placeholder_rev -v $@ -j $< -d "$(CONFIG) testing"

# gen_features.py rules utilizing $(CONFIG)
$(APP_NAME)_features: $(BUILD_DIR)/gen_features.py marble_features.yaml
	$(PYTHON) $< -i $(filter %.yaml, $^) -c $(CONFIG) --split
.PHONY: $(APP_NAME)_features

# ====
# tmds_test, requires access to unisims (or unisims_lrd)
tmds_test_tb: tmds_test.v
VFLAGS_tmds_test_tb = -y $(UNISIMS_DIR)

# =====
# VPI module support for live testing
# https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TUN/TAP
CFLAGS_udp-vpi.o = $(VPI_CFLAGS) -D_POSIX_C_SOURCE=200809L
CFLAGS_udp_model.o = $(VPI_CFLAGS)
udp-vpi.vpi: udp_model.o

# =====
# MMC mailbox self-checking testbench
mmc_mailbox_tb: mmc_mailbox.v $(HOMELESS_DIR)/fake_dpram.v $(DSP_DIR)/dpram.v $(BADGER_DIR)/tests/spi_gate.v $(PERIPH_DRIVERS_DIR)/spi_master.v

# =====
# subset that is interesting to test live on network
AD5662_V = ad5662_lock.v pps_lock.v pps_loop_filter.v ad5662.v
LB_MARBLE_SLAVE_V = lb_marble_slave.v dpram.v reg_tech_cdc.v freq_count.v freq_gcount.v multi_counter.v config_romx.v $(I2CBRIDGE_V) $(AD5662_V) \
                    gps_test.v simpleuart.v fake_dpram.v
LB_MARBLE_SLAVE_VX = xadc_tempvoltmon.v dna.v  # not used in CDC exercise with yosys
lb_marble_slave_tb: $(LB_MARBLE_SLAVE_V) $(LB_MARBLE_SLAVE_VX) mem_gateway.v reg_delay.v client_sub.v i2c_slave_model.v fake_xadc.v udp-vpi.vpi IDDR.v
VFLAGS_lb_marble_slave_tb = -m ./udp-vpi
net_slave_run: lb_marble_slave_tb udp-vpi.vpi
	$(VVP) $< +udp_port=8030 +vcd

# =====
# Actual regression test of the above; tricky!
# Depends on UDP port 8030 not being tied up.
# It would be nice to have a "quiet" option to udp-vpi.

marble.bit:
	echo "dummy" > firmware/app.bin
	$(PYTHON) marble.py --no-compile-gateware --csr-csv csr.csv --cpu-type picorv32 --uart-name crossover
	make -C firmware clean all
	$(PYTHON) marble.py --build --csr-csv csr.csv --cpu-type picorv32 --uart-name crossover+uartbone

load:
	$(PYTHON) marble.py --load --no-compile-gateware --no-compile-software

clean:
	rm -rf build
	rm -f analyzer.csv csr.csv

file.content:
# It would be nice to have a "quiet" option to udp-vpi.
net_slave_check: lb_marble_slave_tb udp-vpi.vpi read_trx.dat
	$(VVP) $< +udp_port=8030 2>/dev/null & sleep 1 && $(PYTHON) testcase.py -a localhost -p 0 --sim --ramtest --stop --rlen=34

# syntax-check only, with no actual test bench code
MARBLE_BASE_V0 = marble_base.v packet_categorize.v data_xdomain.v flag_xdomain.v ltm_sync.v $(RTEFI_V) activity.v base_rx_mac.v spi_gate.v mac_compat_dpram.v ctrace.v $(LB_MARBLE_SLAVE_V) $(FREQ_DEMO_V) mmc_mailbox.v
MARBLE_BASE_V = $(MARBLE_BASE_V0) $(LB_MARBLE_SLAVE_VX)
marble_base_tb: $(MARBLE_BASE_V) fake_xadc.v IDDR.v
VFLAGS_marble_base_tb =

.PHONY: no_multiple_drivers_check
no_multiple_drivers_check: marble_base_tb
	$(AWK) '$$NF=="drivers" && $$(NF-1)>1 {print $$0; code=1}BEGIN{code=0}END{exit(code)}' $<
	@echo "PASS"

marble_base_lint: $(MARBLE_BASE_V) fake_xadc.v IDDR.v
	$(VERILATOR) --lint-only -DSIMULATE $(VLATOR_LINT_IGNORE) $^

# You'd better have a verilator in the v5 series!
marble_top_lint: marble_top.v $(APP_NAME)_features $(MARBLE_BASE_V) fake_xadc.v $(FPGA_FAMILY_DIR)/ds_clk_buf.v $(SERIAL_IO_DIR)/gmii_to_rgmii.v
	$(VERILATOR) --lint-only --timing $(VLATOR_LINT_IGNORE) -DSIMULATE -y $(FPGA_FAMILY_DIR)/xilinx $(filter-out $(APP_NAME)_features, $^)

# =====
# check of basic CDC-correctness
marble_base_yosys.json: marble_base_shell.v $(MARBLE_BASE_V0)
# exercise with make marble_base_cdc.txt

# =====
# constraint files and bitfile generation targets mappings
pin_map_fmc.csv: pin_map_fmc.py
	$(PYTHON) $< > $@

$(CONFIG).xdc: $(BADGER_DIR)/tests/meta-xdc.py $(patsubst %,$(BOARD_SUPPORT_DIR)/$(BOARD_DIR)/%, Marble.xdc pin_map.csv) pin_map_fmc.csv $(BOARD_TOP_CSV)
ifeq ($(CONFIG),marblemini)
	$(PYTHON) $^ > $@
else
	$(PYTHON) $^ | sed -e '/FMC._.[AK]_/s/IOSTANDARD LVCMOS25/IOSTANDARD LVCMOS25 PULLTYPE PULLUP/' > $@
endif

# =====
# Initialization file for i2cbridge
read_trx.dat: read_trx.py
	$(PYTHON) $< > $@

# Build a testbench with Verilator that can be put on the (simulated) network as 192.168.7.4
# marble_base.v is over in board_support/marblemini, but most of the infrastructure,
# including lb_marble_slave.v, is here.
Vmarble_base: $(MARBLE_BASE_V) fake_xadc.v marble_base_sim.cpp ethernet_model.c tap_alloc.c crc32.c
	rm -f ethernet_model.o tap_alloc.o crc32.o  # can't leave C version around for C++ to find
	$(VERILATOR) --trace-fst $(VLATOR_LINT_IGNORE) -DSIMULATE -cc -CFLAGS -Wno-logical-op --exe $^
	MAKEFLAGS="" make -C obj_dir -f Vmarble_base.mk USER_CPPFLAGS="-I $(BADGER_DIR)/tests"
	mv obj_dir/$@ $@
# Recipe:
#  make Vmarble_base read_trx.dat && ./Vmarble_base +trace
#  ping -c 2 192.168.7.4; python3 lbus_access.py -a 192.168.7.4 -t 3 mem 2097200:8; python3 lbus_access.py -a 192.168.7.4 -t 3 reg 327686=1
#  gtkwave marble_base_sim.vcd marble_base_sim.gtkw

# =====
# bitfile
MARBLE_SYNTH_SOURCE = marble_top.v tmds_test.v marble/gmii_clock_handle.v $(FPGA_FAMILY_DIR)/xilinx/xilinx7_clocks.v gmii_to_rgmii.v $(MARBLE_BASE_V) read_trx.dat ../../fpga_family/ds_clk_buf.v

$(CONFIG).d: $(MARBLE_SYNTH_SOURCE) $(CONFIG).xdc
	echo $^ | tr ' ' '\n' > $@

# Target is now $(CONFIG).bit (e.g. marblemini.bit or marble.bit)
$(CONFIG).bit: marble.tcl $(CONFIG).d $(APP_NAME)_features bit_stamp_mod
	$(SYNTH_VIVADO) $< -tclargs $(filter %.d, $^) $(CONFIG) $(APP_NAME) $(BUILD_DIR)/vivado_tcl/swap_gitid.tcl $(GTX_TCL)

.PHONY: bit
bit: $(CONFIG).bit

# =====
# bitfile loading and testing

.PHONY: hwload hwtest
hwload:
	@echo "Loading $(CONFIG).bit onto $(CONFIG)..."
ifeq ($(CONFIG),marblemini)
	openocd -f serial.cfg -f marble.cfg -c "transport select jtag; init; xc7_program xc7.tap; pld load 0 $(CONFIG).bit; exit"
else
	openocd -c "adapter driver ftdi; ftdi_serial 0000$(SERIAL);" -f marble.cfg -c "transport select jtag; init; xc7_program xc7.tap; pld load 0 $(CONFIG).bit; exit"
endif

hwtest: udprtx
	ping -c 2 $(IP_ADDR)
	test $$(PYTHONPATH=../common $(PYTHON) -m leep.cli leep://$(IP_ADDR):803 gitid) = $$(git rev-parse HEAD) && echo "gitid OK"
	./udprtx $(IP_ADDR) 100000 8  # should take under 3 seconds
ifeq ($(CONFIG),marblemini)
	$(PYTHON) testcase.py -a $(IP_ADDR) --trx --marble 0
else
	$(PYTHON) testcase.py -a $(IP_ADDR) --trx --si570
	sn="$$($(PYTHON) zest_sn.py -a $(IP_ADDR) -p 803)" && echo "$$sn" && test "$$sn" = $(ZEST_SN)
endif

dna_tb: dna_tb.v dna.v

clean:
	rm -f $(RTEFI_CLEAN) *_tb *.d *.o *.vpi *.vcd *.pyc i2cbridge/*.pyc
	rm -f pin_map_fmc.csv marble*.xdc marble*.bit marble_features*.vh marble*.json vivado*
	rm -f scalar_marble_regmap.json marble_regmap.json config_romx.v
	rm -f read_trx.dat Vmarble_base bit_stamp_mod udprtx marble_base_cdc.txt
	rm -rf _xilinx .Xil obj_dir __pycache__

1	// rtefi_blob.v is normally a preprocessed version of rtefi_preblob.v with
2	// definitions from the include file expressed/expanded.  That include file
3	// is itself machine-generated based on the desired clients.
4	//
5	// TL;DR: if this file is named rtefi_blob.v, it's probably a mistake to
6	// hand-edit it!
7	//
8	`include "rtefi_preblob.vh"
9	module rtefi_blob #(
10		parameter paw = 11,  // packet (memory) address width, nominal 11
11		parameter n_lat = 8,  // latency of client pipeline
12		parameter mac_aw = 10,  // sets size (in 16-bit words) of DPRAM in Tx MAC
13		// See comments in rtefi_center.v
14		parameter [31:0] ip = {8'd192, 8'd168, 8'd7, 8'd4},  // 192.168.7.4
15		parameter [47:0] mac = 48'h12555500012d,
16	`BLOB_PARAMS
17		parameter udp_port0 = 7,
18		parameter udp_port1 = 801,
19		parameter udp_port2 = 802,
20		parameter udp_port3 = 803,
21		parameter udp_port4 = 804,
22		parameter udp_port5 = 0,
23		parameter udp_port6 = 0,
24		parameter udp_port7 = 0
25	) (
26		// GMII Input (Rx)
27		input rx_clk,
28		input [7:0] rxd,
29		input rx_dv,
30		input rx_er,
31		// GMII Output (Tx)
32		input tx_clk,
33		output [7:0] txd,
34		output tx_en,
35		output tx_er,
36		// Configuration
37		input enable_rx,
38		input config_clk,
39		input [3:0] config_a,
40		input [7:0] config_d,
41		input config_s,  // MAC/IP address write
42		input config_p,  // UDP port number write
43		// Host side of Tx MAC
44		// connect the 2 below to an external 16 bit dual port ram
45		output [mac_aw - 1: 0] host_raddr,
46		input [15:0] host_rdata,
47		// address where we should start transmitting from
48		input [mac_aw - 1: 0] buf_start_addr,
49		// set start to trigger transmission, wait for done, reset start
50		input tx_mac_start,
51		output tx_mac_done,
52		// port to Rx MAC memory
53		output [7:0] rx_mac_d,
54		output [11:0] rx_mac_a,
55		output rx_mac_wen,
56		// port to Rx MAC handshake
57		input rx_mac_hbank,
58		output [1:0] rx_mac_buf_status,
59		// port to Rx MAC packet selector
60		output [7:0] rx_mac_status_d,
61		output rx_mac_status_s,
62		input rx_mac_accept,
63		// Debugging
64		output [3:0] scanner_debug,
65		output ibadge_stb,
66		output [7:0] ibadge_data,
67		output obadge_stb,
68		output [7:0] obadge_data,
69		output xdomain_fault,
70		// Pass-through to user modules
71	`BLOB_PORTS
72		// Dumb stuff to get LEDs blinking
73		output rx_mon,
74		output tx_mon,
75		// Simulation-only
76		output in_use
77	);
78	
79	wire [10:0] len_c;
80	wire [6:0] raw_l;
81	wire [6:0] raw_s;
82	wire [7:0] idata;
83	wire [7*8-1:0] mux_data_in;
84	
85	rtefi_center #(

88		.udp_port0(udp_port0),
89		.udp_port1(udp_port1),
90		.udp_port2(udp_port2),

file.content:
# Defs are made available as Verilog defines
# Params are made available as Verilog localparams and included in ROM metadata

__base_defs: &base_defs
    USE_I2CBRIDGE: 1
    MMC_CTRACE: 1
    GPS_CTRACE: 0

__base_params: &base_params
    default_enable_rx: 1
    misc_config_default: 4
    use_rgmii_idelay: 1

__marble_defs: &marble_defs
    MARBLE_V2: 1
    USE_SI570: 1

__marble_params: &marble_params
    carrier: "Marble"
    carrier_rev: "v2"
    # Options are gt_ref_clk, ddr_ref_clk, or sys_clk
    # sys_clk is only usable if use_rgmii_idelay: 0
    # ddr_ref_clk is recommended over gt_ref_clk to avoid having basic
    #   boot and communications depend on ADN4600 programming
    sysclk_src: "ddr_ref_clk"

marblemini:
    defs:
        <<: *base_defs
        MARBLE_MINI: 1
        USE_SI570: 0
    params:
        <<: *base_params
        carrier: "Marble Mini"
        carrier_rev: "v1"
        # Options are gt_ref_clk or sys_clk
        # sys_clk is only usable if use_rgmii_idelay: 0
        sysclk_src: "gt_ref_clk"

marble:
    defs:
        <<: *base_defs
        <<: *marble_defs
    params:
        <<: *base_params
        <<: *marble_params

content.content:
===badger README head===
# Packet Badger

## Introduction

Packet Badger is a digital logic design, implemented on an FPGA, that
digs through Ethernet packets to construct a response.  Its intended
application is to let workstations and servers communicate with FPGA-based
instruments over UDP.  On its own, it provides ARP, ICMP echo, and UDP echo
services.  It has a (documented and tested) interface to add additional
UDP services that provide the application-useful data flow.

Packet Badger attaches to Ethernet using the GMII standard.  Adapter layers
inside the FPGA can let it connect to GMII, RGMII, SGMII, or MGT PHY hardware.
Mechanisms are provided to attach a software-based simulation of Packet Badger
to the computer's TUN/TAP subsystem (requires root access for setup),
or single clients to a UDP socket, permitting development of client software
and HDL without depending on the target hardware.

Packet Badger is written in portable, synthesizable Verilog.  When targeting
Xilinx 7-series chips, it occupies about 1000 LUTs and 1 RAMB18.

Packet Badger is designed to only respond promptly to packets, never initiate
traffic.  It is tuned for plug-in modules (one per UDP service port) that can
respond in a fixed number (parameterized) of clock cycles latency.  Examples
are given for such modules that:

* Gateway to an on-chip local bus (protocol [documentation](mem_gate.md))
* Give read/write access to an SPI Flash memory

Unlike logic designs that use a soft core to implement Ethernet/IP protocols,
it is capable of full-rate gigabit-per-second data transfer, and has a
relatively small footprint in the FPGA fabric and memory.

This design roughly parallels an earlier LBNL Ethernet fabric design
(PSPEPS), but with architectural bugs fixed.  If the Ethernet physical
link stays up, it will never drop a packet.

The architecture permits adding a MAC for a soft-core CPU, that could be
useful for low-bandwidth setup functions like DHCP or SCPI.  An initial
implementation is included.

The author asserts that its architecture will permit addition of strong
authentication to each packet, without adding (much) overhead in latency,
throughput, or hardware resources.  Efforts to demonstrate that are still
in a prototyping stage, and are not included here.

Only synthesizable code, programs (and their data) to generate synthesizable
code, and documentation are here in the base directory.  Files that implement
the extensive self-test capability, including test builds for hardware, are
squirreled away in the tests/ directory.  Projects that instantiate this
code are expected to accomplish the build step by including rules.mk.

## Block Diagram

![block diagram](doc/rtefi.svg)

## Self-tests

An extensive set of self-tests and demonstrations are programmed up in
the tests subdirectory.  These range from simple exercises for the
input packet scanner to running a TFTP server that uses the MAC feature.
Some of these tests attach the simulated logic to the host network.

The self-tests are run as part of a Continuous Integration (CI) process.
You can also run them on your workstation with a simple "cd tests; make".

## Functionality

Input packets are checked according to the following.
Failures are not reported, just dropped.

All packets:

* Source MAC is unicast, not multicast
* CRC32 OK
* Total GMII frame length (including Ethernet header and CRC32) <= 1536
* Minimum frame length _not_ checked

ARP request:

I now have a thorough, source-grounded understanding of the entire integration story. Let me consolidate the key facts I'll use, with line numbers for permanent links:

**marble_top.v** (projects/test_marble_family/marble_top.v):
- L1-2: comment "Top level Marble-Mini and Marble v2 test build / Mostly cut-and-paste from rgmii_hw_test.v"
- L4: `include "marble_features_defs.vh"` — feature defs from yaml
- L6-101: FPGA pin ports (GTREFCLK, RGMII, SPI to MCU, boot flash, I2C, WR DAC, UART, FMC, LEDs, Pmod)
- L103: `include "marble_features_params.vh"`
- L106-110: localbus wires + `assign lb_din = 32'hfaceface;` (dummy app — localbus currently unused)
- L121: `include "marble_mid.vh"` — the real instantiation
- L112-119: MARBLE_V2 conditional wires

**marble_mid.vh** (projects/test_marble_family/marble_mid.vh):
- L2-20: clock buffering (IBUFDS_GTE2 for GT refclk, BUFG)
- L97-118: xilinx7_clocks MMCM (125MHz → tx_clk, tx_clk90, 200MHz for IDELAY)
- L136-162: gmii_to_rgmii (DDR conversion)
- L219-255: marble_base instantiation — the real work; connects vgmii, SPI, I2C, WR DAC, UART, FMC, and crucially lb_clk/lb_addr/lb_strobe/lb_rd/lb_write/lb_data_out/lb_data_in
- L260: defparam base.rtefi.p4_client... (SPI flash client)
- L264-267: Pmod/LD16/LD17 LED assignment

**marble_base_shell.v** (projects/test_marble_family/marble_base_shell.v):
- L2: comment "Ultra-stupid layer to define clock domains of signals coming in and out of marble_base / See cdc_snitch.py"
- L97-98: `(* magic_cdc *)` attributes on vgmii_rxd_r etc.
- L112-119: register lb outputs in lb_clk domain
- L128-129: `(* magic_cdc *)` on lb_data_in_r, GPS_r
- L136-182: instantiate marble_base (i_mb)
- Note: this shell is NOT used in marble_top.v (marble_top instantiates marble_base directly via marble_mid.vh). Actually wait — let me re-check. marble_mid.vh instantiates `marble_base base(...)` directly (L219), NOT marble_base_shell. So marble_base_shell.v is an alternative CDC-instrumented wrapper. Hmm. Let me note this carefully — the shell exists for cdc_snitch analysis (marble_base_cdc.txt target uses marble_base_shell.v per Makefile L144). So marble_top → marble_base directly; marble_base_shell is the CDC-analysis variant.

**marble_base.v** (board_support/marblemini/marble_base.v) — THE integration:
- L113-120: localbus master signals derived from rtefi_blob: `assign lb_clk = tx_clk; assign lb_strobe = lb_control_strobe; assign lb_write = lb_control_strobe & ~lb_control_rd;` etc.
- L130-153: mmc_mailbox — SPI mailbox to microcontroller, provides config_s/config_p/config_a/config_d for badger's MAC/IP/port config
- L180-211: lb_marble_slave — the application slave (I2C, GPS, FMC test, LEDs, etc.)
- L213-221: address space partitioning: `lb_addr[23:20]==1` → app, `==2` → mailbox, else slave
